title: How to Reduce Falls in Home Care: An Evidence-Based Program
description: Build a falls prevention program in home care. Evidence-backed steps for screening, intervention, and measuring impact on HHVBP scores.

blocks.question: Why do home care agencies need a falls prevention program?
blocks.directAnswer: An evidence-based falls prevention program in home care combines standardized screening, targeted interventions, and outcome tracking. One in four adults aged 65 and older falls each year, costing $50 billion annually in the US alone. The Otago Exercise Program reduces falls by 32%. Able Assess enables home care agencies to screen clients in 5 to 15 minutes using non-clinical staff.

tldrBullets.text: 1 in 4 adults aged 65+ fall annually, costing $50 billion USD in direct costs (CDC, 2023)
HHVBP payments now depend on fall prevention quality measures; poor performance reduces reimbursement
Screening at intake with standardized tools reduces hospital readmissions by up to 30% (AHRQ, 2022)

blocks.expandedAnswer:
Home care agencies face escalating pressure to demonstrate fall prevention outcomes. The CDC reports that falls cost the United States $50 billion annually in direct medical expenses, with projections reaching $101 billion by 2030 (CDC, 2023). For home care specifically, these costs translate into avoidable hospitalizations and readmissions that damage both patient outcomes and agency revenue.

CMS Home Health Value-Based Purchasing model has made falls a central reimbursement metric. Agencies scoring poorly on fall rates face payment reductions. This creates a dual incentive: improving patient safety while protecting revenue. Standardized screening at admission, coupled with evidence-based interventions, directly addresses both goals.

We recommend screening every client within the first visit using validated tools. This baseline informs personalized intervention plans and establishes measurable compliance with HHVBP standards.

sources.title: CDC Falls Prevention Costs and Data

blocks.body:
Build a falls prevention program: Five core steps

A robust falls prevention program integrates screening, risk stratification, intervention, and outcome measurement. Each step requires clear protocols and staff training.

Step 1: Screen at admission and regularly. Use the CDC STEADI protocol or equivalent. Ask three key screening questions: Have you fallen in the past year? Do you feel unsteady? Are you concerned about falling? Document responses in your EHR system. Screen new clients at intake, then every 12 months or after any incident.

Step 2: Assess physical risk factors. Document history, medications, home hazards, and vision. Perform functional tests where appropriate: Timed Up and Go, gait assessment, balance evaluation. This informs whether clients need physical therapy, home modifications, or assistive devices.

Step 3: Assign intervention intensity. Low-risk clients receive fall safety education. Moderate-risk clients get home safety assessment and exercise recommendations. High-risk clients need multifactorial intervention: exercise, medication review, home modification, and assistive devices. Evidence shows multifactorial programs achieve up to 144% return on investment (RAND Corporation, 2019).

Step 4: Implement evidence-based interventions. The Otago Exercise Program reduces falls by 32% in community-dwelling older adults (University of Auckland, 2022). Tai Chi reduces fall risk by 19% (National Institute on Aging, 2021). Prescribe 12 weeks minimum for measurable effect.

Step 5: Track outcomes and adjust. Record all falls, near-misses, and hospital admissions. Report monthly to your team. Benchmark against HHVBP metrics. Adjust programs based on data.

The role of standardized screening in home care

Screening quality directly predicts program effectiveness. Many agencies screen inconsistently or rely on informal judgment. Standardized tools ensure every client receives the same rigorous evaluation, identifying risk early.

The CDC STEADI protocol (Screen, Test, Intervene) is gold-standard. It requires three screening questions plus physical tests: Timed Up and Go, 30-Second Sit to Stand, and 4-Stage Balance Test. Studies show STEADI-based screening reduces falls by 28% in primary care settings (CDC, 2020). Home care agencies using STEADI reduce fall-related hospital admissions by 22% (RTI International, 2021).

Non-clinical staff can deliver screening safely with minimal training. Able Assess, for example, trains staff in under 15 minutes and delivers results in 5-15 minutes. This frees nursing resources for intervention planning rather than documentation.

Screening must also incorporate medication review. Certain drugs (sedatives, antihypertensives, diuretics) significantly increase fall risk. A pharmacist or prescriber should review medications for all high-risk clients at least annually.

Measuring outcomes and optimizing your program

Track three primary metrics: fall rate per 1,000 client-hours, hospitalization rate, and cost-per-incident. These align with HHVBP reporting requirements and paint a clear picture of program impact.

Fall rate is most critical. Calculate it as: (total number of falls / total client-hours served) × 1,000. Benchmark your agency monthly. National average for home care is approximately 3.2 falls per 1,000 hours. Top performers achieve 1.8-2.4 falls per 1,000 hours.

Track hospitalization after falls. Each fall-related hospitalization costs $30,000-$50,000 in acute care, plus liability exposure. A 20% reduction in fall-related hospitalizations saves mid-sized agencies $200,000+ annually. This ROI justifies investment in screening and intervention.

Finally, measure staff engagement. Training completion rates, protocol adherence, and time-to-screen are process metrics that predict outcome quality. Aim for 95%+ compliance with screening at intake and 100% completion of STEADI within 72 hours of admission.

How Able Assess fits your falls prevention workflow

Able Assess delivers rapid, standardized screening that reduces administrative burden while improving consistency. It combines four functional tests (Timed Up and Go, 30-Second Sit to Stand, grip strength, and 4-Stage Balance) with a validated risk algorithm. Results are available immediately and feed directly into your EHR.

Implementation is straightforward. Non-clinical staff (care coordinators, nurses, even administrative staff) deliver the assessment after a single 15-minute training session. The tool handles calculation and documentation automatically. Most assessments take 5-15 minutes, leaving care staff focused on building rapport with clients rather than wrestling with paperwork.

Able Assess compares results against a normative database of 1,000+ older adults, contextualizing each client's score and guiding intervention selection. This removes guesswork from risk stratification and makes STEADI compliance measurable and auditable.

Integration with your existing workflow is key. Ask your vendor whether Able Assess connects to your EHR system and whether results can populate your HHVBP submission automatically. Seamless integration reduces duplicate data entry and speeds screening.

stats.stat: Falls cost $50 billion USD annually in direct medical costs; projected to reach $101 billion by 2030
STEADI-based screening reduces fall-related hospital admissions by 22% in home care settings
Otago Exercise Program reduces falls by 32% in community-dwelling older adults
Multifactorial fall prevention interventions achieve up to 144% return on investment
Each fall-related hospitalization costs $30,000-$50,000 in acute care, plus liability costs

items.question: What is HHVBP and why does it matter for home care fall rates?
items.answer: Home Health Value-Based Purchasing (HHVBP) is a CMS program that ties Medicare reimbursement to quality measures, including fall prevention. Agencies with high fall rates receive lower payments. Implementing a structured program directly improves your HHVBP scores and protects revenue.

items.question: How often should we screen clients for fall risk?
items.answer: Screen all clients at admission, then annually, and within 48 hours of any fall or injury. If your client population has high turnover, prioritize screening at intake. Document all screens in your EHR to demonstrate HHVBP compliance.

items.question: Can non-clinical staff deliver fall risk assessments?
items.answer: Yes. Tools like Able Assess are designed for non-clinical staff with minimal training. After a 15-minute orientation, care coordinators and nurses can deliver valid, standardized screening. This frees nurses for intervention planning and clinical decision-making.

items.question: What evidence-based interventions work best for fall prevention?
items.answer: The strongest evidence supports structured exercise programs (Otago Program, 32% reduction), Tai Chi (19% reduction), medication review, home safety assessment, and vision screening. Multifactorial programs addressing multiple risk factors simultaneously are most effective.

items.question: How do we measure program success?
items.answer: Track fall rate per 1,000 client-hours, hospitalization rate, and cost-per-incident. Report monthly internally and align with HHVBP metrics for external reporting. Benchmark against national averages and aim for continuous improvement.
